channel.title: Drupal Canvas
channel.description: Stay informed about developments in Drupal Canvas.

item.title: #3576578: Inform SDC/code component developers of nonsensical `type: array` prop restrictions: `maxItems <2`
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 6d77b0b · +46/-1 prod · +4/-1 test · 7 files
  • Discussion: #3576578 · 4 contributors · 17 comments

Summary

Canvas now provides clearer error messages when component developers misconfigure array props. Previously, when a component schema defined an array with maxItems set to less than 2, developers received a vague "Nonsensical array size limit specified" error. The new error message explains the specific problem, shows the actual value, and suggests using a non-array type for single-value props. This helps developers understand why arrays must allow at least two items to be useful in Canvas.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

When Canvas added support for type: array props in a previous issue, it included a generic exception for the edge case where maxItems < 2. This made little sense for Canvas because a single-item array is just a pointless wrapper for a component prop, and zero or negative values make even less sense.

This issue improved developer experience by replacing the vague error message with a helpful one. The new message appears in two places: ComponentMetadataRequirementsChecker (which validates component metadata) and JsonSchemaType::computeStorablePropShape() (which interprets JSON schema for storage).

The enhanced message now shows the actual maxItems value received and explicitly suggests using a non-array type for single-value props. A test component called image-gallery-nonsensical was added with maxItems: 1 and a photo of a judging dog to verify the validation works correctly. This pays off technical debt from the initial array support implementation that intentionally left this validation incomplete.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 2, 2026
  • Committed: March 3, 2026 (1 day later)

Key contributors

  • isholgueras (Acquia)
  • velmir_taky
  • wim leers (Acquia)
  • tedbow (Acquia)

Collaboration

This issue resolved quickly, committed within one day of being opened. It was a planned follow-up from a previous array support feature where incomplete validation was intentionally left as technical debt. The maintainer noted this was about "paying off technical debt we accepted to go faster in the summer of last year."

Multiple contributors iterated on the error message wording. The first attempt improved the message, then tedbow refined it further to be more specific. The final version includes the actual maxItems value and actionable guidance. The test component includes a humorous touch with a photo named "gracie-judging.jpg" showing a judgmental dog, which the maintainer appreciated with "😂👏 'judging Gracie'".

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3572881: It's not possible to scroll the Canvas diagonally with touch pad
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 1.1.0
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: ba84193 · +153/-43 prod · +20/-11 test · 5 files
  • Discussion: #3572881 · 3 contributors · 7 comments

Summary

The canvas editor now supports smooth diagonal scrolling with trackpad gestures, matching the natural feel of design tools like Figma. Previously, panning with a trackpad was restricted to horizontal or vertical movements only. The improved gesture handling also provides more natural, continuous zoom behavior during pinch and scroll-to-zoom interactions, replacing the previous stepped zoom levels with fluid scaling based on gesture speed and magnitude.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The editor frame now uses the @use-gesture/react library to handle trackpad and touch gestures more naturally. The previous implementation listened to raw wheel events and processed them in discrete steps, throttling events to 50ms intervals and snapping to predefined zoom levels. This caused jerky diagonal scrolling because horizontal and vertical movements were processed separately.

The new implementation uses useGesture with separate handlers for wheel and pinch events. Free-form panning applies both X and Y deltas simultaneously, enabling true diagonal scrolling. Zoom behavior differs by browser: Chrome and Firefox fire wheel events with ctrlKey=true during trackpad pinch, which the wheel handler processes with continuous scaling based on delta magnitude. Safari fires native GestureEvents, which the pinch handler processes by writing transforms directly to the DOM during the gesture for smooth 60fps updates, then syncing to Redux when complete.

The zoom sensitivity is tuned differently for mouse wheel versus trackpad pinch gestures. Scaling is now logarithmic (proportional to current scale) rather than stepped, providing natural zoom behavior at all levels. The implementation carefully coordinates between gesture state refs and Redux to avoid conflicts between browser-specific event paths.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 11, 2026
  • Committed: March 3, 2026 (19 days later)

Key contributors

  • jessebaker (Acquia)
  • bnjmnm (Acquia)

Collaboration

This issue progressed efficiently from report to fix within three weeks. jessebaker identified the UX gap by comparing to industry-standard design tools. The proposed resolution suggested exploring gesture libraries, which proved to be the right direction. Both lauriii and bnjmnm contributed code to the fork, with the final implementation carefully handling browser differences between Chrome/Firefox and Safari's gesture event models. The solution required tuning sensitivity constants based on manual testing to achieve the right feel, demonstrating the value of iterative UX refinement.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3572847: Add missing validation to component trees being saved into content entities in tests— and simplify `CanvasTestSetup`
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 789db65 · +155/-73 prod · +182/-159 test · 30 files
  • Discussion: #3572847 · 3 contributors · 20 comments

Summary

Canvas tests now validate all component trees before saving them into content entities. Previously, some test fixtures created invalid component trees without validation, which could hide bugs and lead to incorrect reasoning about Canvas behavior. This gap became apparent during Drupal 11.3 testing. The project added validation to CanvasTestSetup and all kernel tests that create component trees, simplified test fixtures by removing unnecessary adapter usage, and extended the custom PHPCS rule to automatically enforce this requirement going forward. This ensures all tests work with valid data and makes future test authoring safer.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

CanvasTestSetup creates test fixtures for Cypress end-to-end tests and many kernel tests. It was creating content entities with component trees but skipping validation, allowing invalid data into the test database. Similarly, some kernel tests like BlockComponentTest::testGetExplicitInput() created nodes with component trees without validating them first.

The fix adds self::assertEntityIsValid() calls throughout the test suite. This new helper method in CanvasKernelTestBase validates both content entities and config entities with helpful output when validation fails. All component tree creation in tests now goes through validation before saving.

The implementation also simplified test fixtures. Many tests used complex StaticPropSource structures and adapters when simple values would suffice. For example, image inputs changed from full entity reference expressions to just ['target_id' => 4]. This makes tests more readable while still exercising the same Canvas functionality.

A custom PHPCS rule now detects manual assertSame([], self::violationsToArray(...)) patterns in CanvasKernelTestBase subclasses and requires using self::assertEntityIsValid() instead. This enforces consistent validation patterns across the test suite.

The work also introduced DataProviderWithComponentTreeTrait::populateActiveComponentVersionPlaceholders() to handle test data that uses placeholder component versions, replacing the more narrowly-focused trait name that only mentioned block components.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 11, 2026
  • Committed: March 3, 2026 (19 days later)

Key contributors

  • wim leers (Acquia)
  • penyaskito (Acquia)
  • phenaproxima (Acquia)

Collaboration

This issue took a straightforward path from identification to commit across three weeks. Wim Leers identified gaps in test validation after #3561392 revealed that some test fixtures created invalid component trees. Penyaskito initially questioned validating all entity creation in tests but agreed after Wim clarified the issue focused on component trees, not unrelated entity fields. Phenaproxima provided thorough reviews and emphasized the unsexy but necessary nature of this work, which significantly improved Canvas testability. The final implementation extended the custom PHPCS rule using AI assistance to enforce validation requirements automatically going forward. The issue uncovered two additional bugs (#3576701, #3576694) during final implementation.

Lessons learned

  • When test fixtures create entities, validate them before saving. Invalid test data leads to incorrect reasoning about failures and can hide legitimate bugs in the system under test. (via wim leers, phenaproxima)
  • When test infrastructure is shared across many tests (like fixtures or setup classes), enforce validation there even for entities tangential to the feature being tested. Simple mistakes in widely-used fixtures skew reasoning across the entire test suite. (via wim leers, phenaproxima)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3574687: Convert `@group foo` to `#[Group(…)]` + other annotations — to restore the ability to run a subset of Canvas' test suite, enforce it using PHPCS
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Major task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 06d7936 · +60/-0 prod · +658/-414 test · +1/-1 infra · 100 files
  • Discussion: #3574687 · 3 contributors · 18 comments

Summary

Canvas' test suite converted all PHPUnit annotations to PHP attributes. PHPUnit 11 stopped recognizing comment-based annotations like @group and @dataProvider, so tests could not be grouped or filtered. After conversion to attributes like #[Group('…')] and #[DataProvider('…')], the test suite can once again run targeted subsets. A new PHPCS sniff enforces attributes going forward.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

PHPUnit 11 dropped support for docblock annotations, requiring a wholesale migration to PHP 8 attributes. Canvas used PHPUnit's --group flag to run test subsets in CI, such as JavaScript component tests or slow tests. Without attributes, --group JavaScriptComponents found no tests.

The conversion touched every annotation PHPUnit reads: @group, @covers, @coversClass, @dataProvider, and @testWith. PHPUnit does not allow mixing annotations and attributes in a single file, so partial migration was impossible. The Rector rule from #3446380 automated the bulk conversion.

One test class required refactoring: PropSourceTest declared @covers on individual test methods, which PHPUnit 11 forbids. That class split into an abstract base and one concrete subclass per prop source, each covering only its own class.

A new PhpunitAnnotationsSniff detects forbidden annotations and points developers to the correct attribute syntax. This sniff can be deleted once Canvas requires Drupal 12, which will enforce attributes core-wide.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 19, 2026
  • Committed: March 2, 2026 (10 days later)

Key contributors

  • wim leers (Acquia)
  • penyaskito (Acquia)
  • gurnoor kaur

Collaboration

Wim Leers opened the issue after discovering CI could no longer filter tests by group. Penyaskito pointed to the Rector automation from #3446380, which provided the conversion script. Gurnoor kaur made the first commit to the merge request. Wim Leers used AI-assisted tooling (Opus 4.6) to generate the PHPCS sniff and perform the bulk refactoring, completing the work in a single day instead of two.

Lessons learned

  • When upgrading to PHPUnit 11+, test annotations like @group, @dataProvider, and @covers must all be converted to PHP attributes in the same file. PHPUnit no longer allows mixing annotations and attributes within a single test class—convert all annotations at once to restore functionality like group-based test filtering. (via wim leers, longwave)
  • When a framework upgrade breaks existing workflows, enforce the new patterns with automated tooling. After manually converting code (e.g., using Rector to migrate annotations to attributes), add linting rules to prevent regressions and guide future contributors toward the correct approach. (via wim leers)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3571917: UI Support for Multi-Value Prop Configuration for Code Components
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 1d5b2a5 · +2702/-180 prod · +3/-1 test · 15 files
  • Discussion: #3571917 · 8 contributors · 38 comments

Summary

The Canvas code component editor now provides a graphical interface for configuring props that accept multiple values. Component builders can choose between unlimited values (with add/remove buttons) or a fixed limit, and can reorder example values via drag-and-drop. This UI supports text, link, image, video, integer, number, date, and list prop types. Boolean and formatted text props remain single-value only. The feature is front-end only and requires dev mode to be visible.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

Previously, component builders had no UI to mark a prop as multi-valued when creating code components in Canvas. This issue adds checkbox controls to enable multi-value mode for compatible prop types. Once enabled, a dropdown lets builders choose between limited (fixed count with numeric input and increment/decrement buttons) or unlimited (dynamic add/remove buttons) modes. Example values render as draggable rows powered by dnd-kit, preserving order during reordering. Type changes reset multi-value state to prevent schema mismatches.

The implementation introduces FormPropTypeArray for simple types (string, integer, number), extends FormPropTypeLink, FormPropTypeDate, and FormPropTypeEnum to handle both single and array modes, and adds PropValuesSortableList for reusable drag-and-drop rows. The UI syncs with Redux state via the allowMultiple, valueMode, limitedCount, items, and example fields on CodeComponentProp. List types automatically cap the limited count at the number of defined enum options. Validation states track per-input validity for links and dates.

The change is gated behind Canvas dev mode and omits backend persistence, which is handled in #3572553. Required field validation for multi-value props is deferred to #3576124. Over 1000 lines of vitest coverage ensure drag-and-drop, mode switching, count adjustment, and value persistence work correctly.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 6, 2026
  • Committed: March 2, 2026 (24 days later)

Key contributors

  • kunal.sachdev (Acquia)
  • utkarsh_33 (Acquia)
  • vipin.mittal18
  • wim leers (Acquia)
  • narendrar (Acquia)

Collaboration

Kunal Sachdev (Acquia) opened the issue and provided design specifications. Utkarsh_33 (Acquia) implemented the initial UI components and drag-and-drop behavior. Vipin Mittal conducted thorough manual testing across prop types and interaction modes. Narendrar (Acquia) performed detailed design and functional reviews over multiple iterations, catching edge cases with list validation, drag handle styling, default value resetting, and limit enforcement. Wim Leers (Acquia) coordinated the merge sequence with dependency #3566541 to avoid test conflicts and ensured the feature aligned with SDC metadata modeling. All contributors were sponsored by Acquia.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3572292: Implement SPA navigation improvements in Publishing UX
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.1.0
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 86ed3b2 · +88/-32 prod · +31/-6 test · 8 files
  • Discussion: #3572292 · 4 contributors · 16 comments

Summary

The Publishing UX interface now provides smoother navigation when working with changes and errors. When publishing fails due to validation errors, clicking the error link navigates to the problem component in the same tab without reloading the page. When discarding changes, the interface updates immediately without a full page refresh, keeping you in context while the system reverts to the published state. These improvements reduce interruptions and make the publishing workflow feel more responsive and modern.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This issue implements two single-page application (SPA) navigation improvements in the Publishing UX canvas editor. First, error links in the publish review modal now use React Router Link components instead of opening new tabs. The error path construction was simplified to relative URLs since navigation stays within the SPA. The target="_blank" attribute was removed so clicking navigates in-place.

Second, discarding pending changes no longer triggers window.location.reload(). Instead, the discard mutation explicitly invalidates relevant Redux Toolkit Query cache tags for layout, content, and pending changes. For the current page, it clears form field values and resets the layout initialization state so the canvas and sidebar reflect refetched published data. For code components, it invalidates the component cache and sets a forceRefresh flag that bypasses the normal skip logic in useGetCodeEditorData, forcing the editor to refetch canonical data and re-initialize.

The changes preserve staged changes during error navigation and maintain proper state synchronization after discards by selectively clearing cached form values for both component instance and entity forms. End-to-end tests verify SPA navigation behavior and confirm the URL path remains unchanged after discards.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 9, 2026
  • First commit: February 13, 2026 (4 days later)
  • Last commit: February 26, 2026 (13 days later)

Collaboration

lauriii made the first commit implementing both SPA navigation improvements. mayur-sose conducted thorough quality assurance testing with a detailed test matrix covering seven scenarios including focus behavior, state preservation, and browser history support. The QA process identified edge cases around data synchronization after discards that were addressed in a follow-up commit. jessebaker reported the issue and provided clear guidance on workflow expectations when QA tests revealed gaps.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3574668: User has no way of knowing that a required prop needs an example value
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 1.1.0
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 912a8c6 · +387/-41 · 8 files
  • Discussion: #3574668 · 3 contributors · 15 comments

Summary

Component props marked as required now automatically receive sensible example values when the required toggle is activated. If someone clears that example value, the interface displays a validation error explaining that required props need example values. This prevents confusion about configuration requirements and helps authors create valid component definitions on the first attempt. The system provides appropriate defaults for each data type, such as "Example text" for text fields or "0" for numbers.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This change addresses a usability gap in the component props configuration form. Previously, users could mark a prop as required without realizing they also needed to provide an example value, leading to invalid configurations.

The implementation introduces a useRequiredProp custom hook that tracks when the required toggle changes state. When activated on a prop with no example, the hook triggers a callback to prefill an appropriate default based on the prop type. Default values are defined in DEFAULT_EXAMPLES (text: "Example text", integer: "0", date: "2026-01-25", etc.) and DEFAULT_ENUM_OPTIONS for list types.

Each form component (text fields, formatted text, links, dates, and enums) was updated to accept a required prop, integrate the hook, and display validation errors. The error state is managed locally within each form component and synchronized with user input: clearing an example on a required prop shows the error, filling it hides the error, and toggling required off clears the error.

Type changes on required props also trigger prefilling of the appropriate default for the new type. For list types without existing options, the system creates a default option alongside the example value.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 19, 2026
  • Committed: February 25, 2026 (5 days later)

Key contributors

  • hooroomoo (Acquia)
  • mayur-sose
  • wim leers (Acquia)
  • mglaman (Acquia)
  • jptaranto

Collaboration

hooroomoo drove this issue from identification through implementation, including creating the animated demonstration of the behavior. wim leers provided encouraging feedback and specifically thanked hooroomoo for including visual documentation to make the change easy to review.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3573867: Start prototyping Canvas Workbench
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 3a25554 · +7221/-82 prod · +1/-0 infra · 65 files
  • Discussion: #3573867 · 5 contributors · 11 comments

Summary

Canvas Workbench is a new local developer tool that lets you preview and work with Canvas Code Components and pages directly from your source code. Similar to Storybook, it automatically discovers components from your codebase, reads their configuration from component.yml files, and shows you live previews with example values. It includes hot module reload support and uses json-render to preview entire Canvas pages locally without needing a Drupal backend running.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This prototype introduces three new packages. The @drupal-canvas/discovery package scans directories for component.yml and *.component.yml files, respects .gitignore rules, and discovers both components (with JavaScript and optional CSS entries) and pages (from pages/*.json files). It resolves JavaScript entries by extension precedence (.ts, .tsx, .js, .jsx) and generates stable component IDs using SHA1 hashes.

The @drupal-canvas/vite-compat package provides shared Vite compatibility helpers for host project imports. It resolves @/ alias imports from host components, supports asset imports (images, SVGs, CSS), enables SVGR for SVG components, and handles extensionless directory imports with automatic index file resolution.

The @drupal-canvas/workbench package provides the Workbench UI itself. It runs a Vite dev server that serves both the component browser UI and preview iframes. Discovery middleware exposes component and page data at /__canvas/discovery and preview manifests at /__canvas/preview-manifest. The preview system imports component modules through Vite @fs URLs and requires a default export from each component module. A hardcoded Tailwind entrypoint at src/components/global.css is loaded through a virtual CSS module.

The drupal-canvas package gains new json-render utilities. renderSpec() renders json-render specs using a component registry, resolving $state expressions and conditional logic. defineComponentRegistry() builds registries by dynamically importing component JavaScript entries. defineComponentCatalog() converts component metadata to json-render catalogs with Zod schemas for validation and AI prompt generation. Canvas-specific prop normalization unwraps rich text {value, format} objects and image metadata wrappers.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 16, 2026
  • Committed: February 25, 2026 (8 days later)

Key contributors

  • balintbrews (Acquia)
  • wotnak (Smartbees)

Collaboration

This prototype was opened on February 16, 2026 by balintbrews and committed nine days later on February 25. The issue establishes an experimental local development tool to explore Canvas component previewing outside a full Drupal environment. Effulgentsia organized it under the Canvas IDE meta issue and praised the work. Catch raised concerns about namespace conflicts with the existing Workbench contrib module, noting the potential for user confusion despite no technical namespace collision (the package uses @drupal-canvas/workbench in npm). Effulgentsia opened a follow-up issue to decide if the name should change. The prototype introduces substantial infrastructure: a discovery system, Vite compatibility layer, and preview runtime that together demonstrate feasibility of a Storybook-like experience for Canvas components.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3573996: Add utils for converting between Canvas component trees and json-render
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 39de79b · +995/-1 · 6 files
  • Discussion: #3573996 · 4 contributors · 9 comments

Summary

Canvas pages can now be rendered and edited in local development environments using json-render, a library that renders React components from JSON specifications. This enables developers to work on Canvas pages outside of Drupal, using familiar tools like IDEs and version control. The new utilities convert between Canvas's flat component tree format (where components are linked by parent IDs) and json-render's nested specification format, making it possible to prototype Canvas workbenches and editing experiences in standalone React applications.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

Canvas stores component trees as flat arrays where each node references its parent by UUID. The json-render library expects a nested specification with a single root element and slots. Three new utilities bridge these formats.

canvasTreeToSpec() walks the flat Canvas array and reconstructs parent-child relationships into json-render's nested structure. When multiple root components exist, it wraps them in a synthetic canvas:component-tree element since json-render requires a single root. Named slots map directly; the default children slot maps to json-render's special children array.

specToCanvasTree() reverses this conversion. It traverses the json-render spec depth-first, generating Canvas nodes with parent UUIDs and slot names. It preserves existing UUIDs when round-tripping and strips the synthetic wrapper element to restore multi-root trees.

renderCanvasTree() combines conversion with rendering using @json-render/react. A fallback component handles the synthetic wrapper transparently and logs warnings for unknown component types.

These utilities depend on a forked version of json-render that supports named slots (proposed in upstream PR #105), installed via npm aliases until the feature merges upstream. The implementation includes comprehensive round-trip tests validating structure preservation, slot ordering, and edge cases like orphaned components.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 17, 2026
  • Committed: February 25, 2026 (7 days later)

Key contributors

  • wotnak (Smartbees)
  • balintbrews (Acquia)
  • effulgentsia (Acquia)

Collaboration

wotnak implemented the conversion utilities with careful attention to edge cases and comprehensive test coverage, including round-trip validation and error handling for malformed trees. balintbrews provided context about the broader architectural vision, clarifying that UI demonstrations would come later when the workbench prototype materialized. effulgentsia organized the work within the larger IDE integration meta issue, helping connect this foundation to the project roadmap. The issue demonstrates thoughtful API design for a feature that will enable new development workflows.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3575072: Component preview doesn't work for code components in Safari
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 0522ab2 · +172/-30 · 1 file
  • Discussion: #3575072 · 3 contributors · 9 comments

Summary

Component previews in Safari were broken due to authentication issues. When hovering over code components in the library, Safari would show an HTTP 401 error and authentication prompt instead of the preview. This was caused by iframes lacking the authentication context needed for assets that require session information. The fix changes how previews are rendered so they inherit the current session, making previews work correctly in Safari and other browsers.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The component preview system creates iframes to display component markup. Previously, it generated HTML content, created a blob URL with URL.createObjectURL(), and set that as the iframe's src attribute. In Safari, iframes loaded from blob URLs do not share the authentication context of the parent page. Code components include assets that require authentication (like api/v0/auto-save endpoints), causing 401 errors.

The fix switches from src with a blob URL to srcDoc with inline HTML content. Iframes using srcDoc inherit the parent page's security context and session, allowing authenticated requests to succeed. The solution also converts relative asset URLs (in <link>, <script>, and <canvas-island> tags) to absolute URLs so they load correctly.

An additional enhancement watches for size changes in components with <canvas-island> elements. These can change dimensions after hydration, so an interval checks every 250ms for up to 5 seconds and recalculates scaling if the size changes. This ensures previews remain properly scaled even after client-side hydration completes.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 22, 2026
  • Committed: February 24, 2026 (1 day later)

Key contributors

  • lauriii (Acquia)
  • bnjmnm (Acquia)
  • jessebaker (Acquia)

Collaboration

bnjmnm fixed the core authentication issue by switching to srcDoc and added logic to handle dynamic resizing of canvas-island components during hydration. lauriii reported the issue with clear reproduction steps and screenshots that made the Safari-specific problem easy to understand.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3575055: Missing Documentation for Link Prop
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 9aefa52 · +30/-0 · 1 file
  • Discussion: #3575055 · 2 contributors · 7 comments

Summary

The Drupal Canvas documentation now includes guidance on defining link properties in Single Directory Components. The new documentation explains how to use the uri and uri-reference formats in component schemas to accept absolute URLs or relative paths. This helps component developers understand when to use each format and how to validate link inputs properly.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The Canvas documentation site adds a new "Link" section to the props documentation page. This section explains how to use JSON Schema's format: uri and format: uri-reference properties for link validation in Single Directory Component schemas.

The uri format requires absolute URLs with a scheme, like https://example.com or internal:/node/1. The uri-reference format accepts both absolute URLs and relative paths like /about or ../page.html. The documentation includes YAML schema examples for both formats and a Twig usage example showing how to render the link value in a component template.

This is documentation-only and does not change any code behavior. It helps developers choose the appropriate format constraint when authoring component schemas that accept link properties.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 22, 2026
  • Committed: February 23, 2026

Key contributors

  • peterrubin
  • lauriii (Acquia)

Collaboration

peterrubin identified the documentation gap. lauriii added the missing documentation explaining the difference between uri and uri-reference formats with practical examples for component developers.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3574994: Resizable right sidebar for Drupal Canvas
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 29cdd8c · +388/-170 prod · +5/-0 test · 16 files
  • Discussion: #3574994 · 2 contributors · 7 comments

Summary

The right sidebar in Drupal Canvas can now be resized by dragging a handle. Users can adjust the sidebar width to fit their workflow and screen size. The chosen width is remembered across sessions. This improves the editing experience when working with component properties that need more space or when maximizing the preview area.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The implementation replaces the react-resizable-panels library with allotment for both the editor right sidebar and the code editor layout. The new library provides better control over pane visibility and sizing constraints.

In the main editor, the Editor component now wraps the editor frame and ContextualPanel in an Allotment component. The sidebar pane has constraints: SIDEBAR_MIN_PX (320px) minimum, SIDEBAR_MAX_PX (640px) maximum, and a default of 24% width. The pane is hidden when no component is selected in the template editor or when in interactive mode, using the visible prop rather than CSS display none.

The code editor similarly uses nested Allotment components for horizontal and vertical splits. Both layouts persist their sizes to localStorage using new layoutStorage.ts helpers (getLayoutItem and setLayoutItem). These helpers catch storage errors gracefully so the UI falls back to defaults if localStorage is unavailable.

The ContextualPanel CSS changed from a fixed width to flex-based sizing to work within the resizable pane. The layout token --sidebar-right-width was removed since width is now dynamic. Playwright tests were updated to wait for the contextual panel to mount after component selection, as the Allotment pane visibility transitions can delay rendering.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 21, 2026
  • Committed: February 22, 2026 (1 day later)

Key contributors

  • lauriii (Acquia)
  • balintbrews (Acquia)
  • jessebaker (Acquia)
  • mayur-sose

Collaboration

balintbrews made their first commit to the Canvas project with this feature. lauriii opened the issue and provided guidance on the implementation approach.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3566433: `Component::getVersions()` must cast all version hashes to string: 1 in 4 million probability that it fails due to all-digit version hash
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 947eba8 · +18/-1 · 1 file
  • Discussion: #3566433 · 4 contributors · 16 comments

Summary

In rare cases (about 1 in 4 million), component version hashes could be treated as integers instead of strings, causing fatal errors when saving components. This happened when a hash consisted entirely of digits, because PHP automatically converts all-digit array keys from strings to integers. The bug was discovered when a user encountered a fatal type error after adding a component property and clearing the cache. The fix ensures all version hashes are explicitly cast to strings.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The VersionedConfigEntityBase::getVersions() method returns version hashes that are used as array keys throughout the Canvas module. Version hashes are generated using hash('xxh64', json_encode($data)), which produces a 16-character hexadecimal string.

When a hash consists entirely of digits (0-9 rather than including any A-F characters), PHP automatically converts it to an integer when used as an array key. This creates a type mismatch because Component::loadVersion() expects a string parameter, and the component schema defines versions as strings.

The probability of an all-digit hash is (10/16)^16, or approximately 1 in 4 million, because 10 of the 16 hexadecimal characters are digits. The fix adds an array_map() call in getVersions() that explicitly casts all version values to strings, preventing PHP's automatic integer conversion. While other methods like getActiveVersion() and getLoadedVersion() already had explicit string return types, getVersions() was returning raw array keys that could be integers.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 8, 2026
  • Committed: February 19, 2026 (1 month later)

Key contributors

  • interx (Anvil)
  • wim leers (Acquia)
  • penyaskito (Acquia)

Collaboration

interx discovered this extremely rare edge case and investigated the root cause, tracing the problem to PHP's automatic type conversion of all-digit strings used as array keys. wim leers quickly identified the specific method that needed fixing and provided a patch within hours of the report. penyaskito committed the fix to ensure it would be merged.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3568602: Handle upgrading and rendering not yet upgraded component instances of components with new(ly) required props that include an example value
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: ac202d3 · +101/-46 prod · +286/-59 test · 21 files
  • Discussion: #3568602 · 5 contributors · 31 comments

Summary

Canvas now automatically handles component evolution when components add new required properties or change existing properties from optional to required. When a component is updated to require new data fields, existing pages and templates using that component will render with the example values defined in the component's schema. When editors open those pages, Canvas upgrades the component instances automatically, preserving any existing user-provided values while populating new required fields with their defaults. This allows component developers to evolve their designs without breaking published content.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This feature extends the automatic component instance upgrading system introduced in #3567413 to handle two additional evolution scenarios: when a component adds a new required prop with an example value, and when an existing optional prop becomes required.

Canvas assumes every required prop has at least one example value, enforced by ComponentMetadataRequirementsChecker. When a component evolves, the first example value becomes the default_value in the Component entity's prop_field_definitions.

The implementation operates in two contexts. At edit time, GeneratedFieldExplicitInputUxComponentInstanceUpdater::update() now populates new required props with their default values when upgrading component instances. For optional-to-required transitions, it preserves existing user-provided values and only adds defaults when no value was stored. At render time, ComponentTreeItemList::getHydratedTree() ensures not-yet-upgraded component instances can render by calling ComponentSourceInterface::hydrateComponent() with a new $active_required_explicit_inputs parameter containing the default values for all required props in the active version.

The ComponentSourceInterface::getDefaultExplicitInput() method now accepts an $only_required parameter to retrieve only required inputs. The hydrateComponent() method signature adds $active_required_explicit_inputs to provide defaults at render time. These changes apply to all component sources including JsComponent, SingleDirectoryComponent, and BlockComponent.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 20, 2026
  • Committed: February 18, 2026 (29 days later)

Key contributors

  • effulgentsia (Acquia)
  • lauriii (Acquia)
  • penyaskito (Acquia)
  • wim leers (Acquia)
  • mglaman (Acquia)

Collaboration

effulgentsia designed the approach and opened the issue, building on the foundation laid in #3567413. mglaman implemented the core logic for both edit-time upgrades and render-time hydration, including extensive test coverage that validates the behavior across multiple evolution scenarios. penyaskito reviewed the implementation and helped refactor the render logic. Wim Leers provided detailed code review, catching incorrect comments and ensuring the implementation remained clear for future iteration. The team split out related validation concerns to #3574120 to keep this focused on the automatic upgrade behavior.

Lessons learned

  • When rendering component instances that reference older versions of a component definition, apply defaults from the active definition at render time rather than blocking rendering. This allows published content to continue rendering after non-breaking component changes, with the instance data upgraded to the new version when explicitly edited. (via effulgentsia, lauriii, wim leers, mglaman, penyaskito)
  • When a plugin or component definition adds a new required property, guarantee that a safe default exists (such as an example value in the schema). This ensures that existing stored instances can continue to function without migration when the new property is added at render time. (via effulgentsia, wim leers)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3571997: CI: auto-retry known random Cypress E2E failures + PHPUnit's dreaded "RecursiveDirectoryIterator failed to open directory" (since Drupal 11.2) + ComponentAudit DB-dependent result order + Playwright flakiness
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Critical task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: db17f6d · +121/-5 prod · +21/-5 test · +91/-5 infra · 12 files
  • Discussion: #3571997 · 4 contributors · 52 comments

Summary

Canvas has struggled with random test failures in continuous integration for months, forcing developers to manually retry failed jobs over and over. Most failures were not real bugs but symptoms of race conditions in upstream dependencies like PHPUnit and flaky end-to-end tests. This change automatically retries tests when known random failures occur, dramatically reducing friction for contributors. The CI pipeline now catches transient failures on the first run and retries only the affected jobs, letting real failures surface faster without manual intervention.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This change adds automatic retry logic to Canvas's GitLab CI pipeline for PHPUnit, Cypress, and Playwright test jobs. The implementation uses GitLab CI's retry feature with a custom exit code (112) triggered when specific failure patterns are detected.

For PHPUnit tests, the script section was duplicated from the upstream gitlab_templates project's .phpunit-base to inject retry detection logic at the end. The script greps PHPUnit's XML output for RecursiveDirectoryIterator errors that have plagued tests since Drupal 11.2, then exits with code 112 to trigger a retry. The job configuration specifies retry: max: 2, exit_codes: 112 to automatically rerun up to twice when that exit code appears.

Similar patterns were added for Cypress E2E tests, which pipe output to a log file and grep for specific failing test filenames like global-regions-interact.cy.js. Playwright tests detect 1 failed in the output (indicating a single-browser failure rather than a systemic issue across all three browsers) and trigger the same retry mechanism.

The change also includes fixes for the underlying causes where possible. It forward-ports a fix from core issue #2862699 for config entity query multi-sort bugs into Canvas via ConfigEntityQuery and ConfigEntityQueryFactory classes. It adds secondary sorting by ID to API controllers listing config entities to ensure deterministic ordering across databases. Test fixtures were expanded to catch database-dependent sort order issues.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 6, 2026
  • First commit: February 10, 2026 (3 days later)
  • Last commit: February 19, 2026 (9 days later)

Collaboration

Wim Leers drove this work after months of manually retrying failed CI jobs on Canvas. Jonathan Haynes (jonathan1055) provided early review and suggestions for working with the upstream gitlab_templates project, including the idea to make the exit code handling overridable upstream. Penyaskito (Ramón Vilar) reviewed the database sorting fixes and connected them to similar work in #3562563. Jesse Baker and Balint Brennan provided input on the Playwright retry thresholds.

Lessons learned

  • When CI jobs suffer from intermittent infrastructure-level failures (filesystem race conditions, flaky tests), auto-retry on specific failure patterns instead of manual retries. Use after_script to grep structured output (XML, logs) for known signatures, then exit with a custom code (0-255) that triggers GitLab CI's retry: when: script_failure mechanism. This keeps pipelines unblocked while upstream fixes arrive. (via wim leers)
  • When database queries return results in non-deterministic order (due to lack of explicit sort or ambiguous sort keys like identical timestamps), tests that assert exact array equality will randomly fail across database engines. Always sort query results by a stable, unique key (like entity ID) in PHP if the database-level sort is insufficient, especially when the performance cost is negligible. (via wim leers, penyaskito)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3568906: Handle upgrading and rendering not yet upgraded component instances of components with newly removed props or slots
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 6107979 · +74/-29 prod · +370/-25 test · 8 files
  • Discussion: #3568906 · 5 contributors · 37 comments

Summary

Canvas now automatically handles component evolution when developers remove props or slots from components. When a site builder edits a page containing an old component instance, the system removes data for props and slots that no longer exist. This prevents orphaned data from accumulating and ensures pages remain valid even as components change over time. If a developer removes a prop or slot and then adds it back before the page is edited, the old data is preserved and continues to work.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The GeneratedFieldExplicitInputUxComponentInstanceUpdater class now handles removed props and slots as safe upgrade operations. Previously, removing props or slots was considered unsafe and blocked automatic upgrades. Now when canUpdate() evaluates a component instance, it no longer checks for removed props or slots. The update() method uses array_diff_key() to identify removed props and deletes their values from the component instance inputs. For removed slots, it filters the ComponentTreeItemList to remove child component instances whose slot name matches a removed slot and whose parent UUID matches the component being upgraded.

The ComponentSourceManager::updateComponentInstances() method now returns a boolean indicating whether any instances were modified. ApiLayoutController::buildRegion() uses this return value to determine whether to create an auto-save entity. When the controller detects modifications during GET requests, it saves the updated component tree as an auto-save so subsequent PATCH requests work with the cleaned-up tree rather than the published version.

Test coverage includes ApiLayoutControllerPatchTest::testPatchCleansUpOrphanedChildrenOnComponentEvolution(), which verifies a two-phase removal scenario: first removing one slot from a component with two slots, then removing the remaining slot. The test confirms that children in removed slots are cleaned up during auto-update and that publishing succeeds after each phase.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 22, 2026
  • First commit: February 13, 2026 (22 days later)
  • Last commit: February 17, 2026 (3 days later)

Key contributors

  • effulgentsia (Acquia)
  • wim leers (Acquia)
  • lauriii (Acquia)
  • mglaman (Acquia)
  • penyaskito (Acquia)

Collaboration

Matt Glaman implemented the core logic for removing orphaned props and slots, including the elegant solution for filtering component trees to remove slot children. Penyaskito provided detailed steps to reproduce edge cases and created follow-up issues to track UX improvements around auto-saves. Effulgentsia wrote a comprehensive issue summary that clearly scoped the problem and guided the implementation. Wim Leers provided thorough code review, raising important questions about data loss and helping refine the approach through multiple iterations. Lauri Eskola confirmed the product direction, balancing simplicity against potential concerns about content loss.

Lessons learned

  • When component schemas evolve, delaying data cleanup until edit-time provides a recovery window. If schema changes are reverted before content is edited, the old data resurfaces automatically without requiring restoration logic. (via effulgentsia, lauriii, mglaman)
  • When rendering components with schema mismatches, rendering with extraneous data (extra props or slots that no longer exist in the schema) is safer than filtering it out. The component code simply ignores unexpected data, avoiding the need for runtime transformation logic and allowing cleanup to happen explicitly during edit operations. (via mglaman, penyaskito, effulgentsia)
  • When removing features that contain user data (like component slots or fields), follow established deletion patterns from analogous systems. Just as deleting a folder deletes its contents or removing a database column deletes its data, removing a container should remove its contents rather than attempting to rescue orphaned data into visible UI elements. (via lauriii)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3550848: Linker works for `type: string, format: date-time`, but `DateTimeDefaultWidget` renders the linker twice
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 789863d · +46/-5 · 5 files
  • Discussion: #3550848 · 3 contributors · 11 comments

Summary

When a datetime field was linked to a component property in Canvas, the linker icon appeared twice – once for the date input and once for the time input. This happened because the datetime widget uses a fieldset with two separate input fields, and the system was attaching a linker to each input's label instead of to the fieldset's legend. The fix ensures only one linker appears at the fieldset level, making the interface cleaner and easier to understand. The change also revealed and fixed a related issue where datetime values were stored without timezone information, preventing linked datetime properties from rendering correctly.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The datetime field widget (DateTimeDefaultWidget) renders two input elements inside a fieldset – one for date and one for time. The linker system was designed to attach to form input labels, so it created two linkers for this single field.

The fix modifies GeneratedFieldExplicitInputUxComponentSourceBase::processElementTreeLinkerLabels() to detect when a form element has a fieldset theme wrapper and passes the prop link data to the fieldset via a new #prop_link_data property. The canvas_stark_preprocess_fieldset() function then JSON-encodes this data and makes it available to the template.

The fieldset.html.twig template was updated to use <canvas-drupal-label> in the legend instead of a plain <span>, allowing it to render the linker when prop_link_data is present. This approach matches the existing solution for the media library widget.

The DrupalFormElementLabel.tsx component was also updated to respect titleDisplay !== 'invisible' before rendering linkers, preventing linkers from appearing for invisible labels.

The fix surfaced a separate bug where datetime values stored without timezone information failed JSON Schema validation. A workaround was added to Evaluator::evaluate() for entity field expressions, appending Z to datetime strings that lack timezone information. This matches the existing workaround for field type expressions. The root cause is tracked separately.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: October 7, 2025
  • Committed: February 17, 2026 (4 months later)

Collaboration

Wim Leers opened the issue and implemented the fix, resolving a merge conflict with existing fieldset template changes from the media library work. bnjmnm identified the root cause early in the issue, explaining that linkers associate with form input labels but datetime widgets use fieldset legends instead, and noting that similar special-casing was already needed for media widgets.

Lessons learned

  • When rendering UI elements for compound widgets (like date/time pickers with multiple inputs), attach interactive metadata to the container's primary label rather than to each individual input. Attaching to every input creates duplicate UI affordances that confuse users. (via bnjmnm)
  • When a bug is hidden behind another bug, applying an existing workaround to the newly exposed scenario enables pragmatic progress. Once the system works end-to-end, the root cause can be addressed separately without blocking users. (via wim leers)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3573796: `json-schema-definitions://canvas.module/stream-wrapper-uri` known prop shape is incorrectly mapped to link field type, causing error
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 5f829be · +31/-0 prod · +72/-0 test · 6 files
  • Discussion: #3573796 · 2 contributors · 16 comments

Summary

Canvas was crashing when Single Directory Components used a JSON schema definition for file URIs (like public://documents/report.pdf) without specifying an image content type. The system incorrectly tried to handle these as link fields, which don't support Drupal's stream wrapper schemes. This meant components that wanted to reference files like PDFs or documents would fail with errors. The fix maps these file URI properties to the correct field type so components can safely reference non-image files stored in Drupal.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The json-schema-definitions://canvas.module/stream-wrapper-uri JSON schema definition specifies a URI with x-allowed-schemes: [public] but no contentMediaType. The JsonSchemaStringFormat::computeStorablePropShape() method was falling through to the default case and mapping these to a link field type with a url property.

When Canvas tried to evaluate example values like public://documents/report.pdf, the LinkUrl::getValue() method called Url::fromUri('public://...'), which fails because stream wrapper URIs are not valid for Url::fromUri(). Only HTTP(S) and a few other specific schemes are supported by that method.

The fix adds a new match condition in JsonSchemaStringFormat::computeStorablePropShape() that checks for x-allowed-schemes containing public and no contentMediaType. When matched, it returns a ReferenceFieldTypePropExpression that maps to a file field type's entity property, then follows the reference to the File entity's uri base field and its value property. This mirrors the existing pattern for stream-wrapper-image-uri but without the image-specific handling. The field widget is set to file_generic.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 16, 2026
  • Committed: February 17, 2026

Key contributors

  • fago (drunomics)
  • wim leers (Acquia)

Collaboration

Fago reported this bug while implementing support for all Canvas JSON schema types in canvas_extjs, discovering the crash through test Vue components. Wim Leers quickly added test cases and expectations, then realized he had previously added test coverage for this exact prop shape in a different issue but had only partially diagnosed the problem. Fago implemented the matching logic to handle the stream wrapper URIs correctly, and both contributors collaborated in real-time as changes appeared in their shared branch.

Lessons learned

  • When mapping generic URI formats to field types, validate that the chosen field type's API supports the URI scheme before committing to the mapping. Stream wrapper URIs (like public://) are not compatible with Drupal's Url::fromUri(), which expects standard web protocols, causing runtime crashes when link field types attempt to process them. (via fago, wim leers)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3561392: Test Canvas on Drupal 11.3
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Critical task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: e52a1ea · +160/-50 prod · +344/-108 test · +13/-32 infra · 102 files
  • Discussion: #3561392 · 5 contributors · 42 comments

Summary

Canvas now fully supports Drupal 11.3, ensuring the site builder works correctly with the latest Drupal core release. This update includes fixes for upstream changes in Drupal core, addresses new deprecations in Symfony 7.4, and adjusts test expectations to account for changes in block plugin configuration between Drupal 11.2 and 11.3. The Canvas continuous integration system now tests both 11.2 and 11.3 to maintain compatibility across versions.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This issue added comprehensive Drupal 11.3 compatibility testing and fixed multiple failures caused by upstream changes. The primary driver of changes was core issue #3547808, which made the label_display block configuration value non-translatable and changed its config schema from type: label to type: string with Choice and NotBlank constraints. This altered component version hashes throughout Canvas because ComponentSourceBase::generateVersionHash() includes the schema definitions in normalized data.

The team updated hundreds of test fixtures to change label_display values from boolean FALSE to string '0' to match the new schema. Tests in ComponentInputsEvolutionTest required special handling because component version hashes differ between 11.2 and 11.3 due to the config schema changes. The solution uses match statements to provide version-specific expected hashes.

Additional changes addressed Symfony 7.4 deprecations by updating constraint instantiation from shorthand notation to explicit array syntax (e.g., addConstraint('StringSemantics', ['semantic' => StringSemanticsConstraint::PROSE]) instead of passing the constant directly). The #[RunTestsInSeparateProcesses] attribute was added to 80+ kernel and functional tests to prevent class redeclaration errors when running the full test suite on 11.3.

The .gitlab-ci.yml now includes a dedicated phpunit (11.3) job that runs on every merge request, ensuring Canvas doesn't regress on 11.3 support. The original phpunit job continues testing 11.2 as Canvas's minimum supported version.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: December 4, 2025
  • First commit: January 7, 2026 (1 month later)
  • Last commit: February 16, 2026 (1 month later)

Key contributors

  • wim leers (Acquia)
  • penyaskito (Acquia)
  • phenaproxima (Acquia)
  • isholgueras (Acquia)

Collaboration

phenaproxima shepherded this complex compatibility effort over six weeks, methodically working through test failures caused by upstream changes and figuring out the root causes of obscure hash mismatches in version tracking. penyaskito provided the breakthrough for handling version-dependent test expectations by implementing core version detection with match statements, then completed the painful merge when conflicts accumulated. Wim Leers conducted thorough review of all 150 changed files to ensure no test coverage was lost and created two follow-up issues to address systemic problems discovered during the work.

Lessons learned

  • When maintaining compatibility across multiple major versions of a dependency, testing against development branches introduces CI instability from upstream changes. Pin to concrete release tags to ensure reproducible builds and avoid unexpected breakage. (via wim leers)
  • When testing infrastructure code (like component versioning), avoid testing with invalid input data. Validation would prevent invalid data in production, so testing against it obscures real issues and wastes debugging time. (via wim leers)
  • When abstracting away default values from users, don't store those defaults in the database. Omit them during save and restore just-in-time to reduce storage overhead and simplify upgrades when defaults change. (via wim leers)
  • When a system generates hashes from configuration that includes schema definitions, upstream schema changes (like new constraints or type refinements) will change those hashes even if the data remains identical. Version-specific test assertions or normalization strategies are needed to handle cross-version compatibility. (via penyaskito, wim leers)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3573480: Add agent skills compatibility setup in @drupal-canvas/create
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 53baa49 · +1286/-6 · 11 files
  • Discussion: #3573480 · 3 contributors · 10 comments

Summary

The Drupal Canvas project scaffolder now helps developers set up compatibility for multiple coding agents when creating a new project. When a template includes agent skills, the scaffolder prompts which AI coding assistants you use and automatically creates the necessary directory links so each agent can find the skills it needs. This solves a compatibility problem where different coding agents expect skills in different directories, even though .agents/skills is becoming the standard location.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The @drupal-canvas/create package now detects when a cloned template repository contains an .agents/skills directory. If found, it calls the new setupAgentSkills function which prompts users to select which coding agents they use from a searchable multiselect interface.

The implementation introduces an agents.ts registry defining 44 coding agents and their expected skills directory paths. Agents are categorized as "universal" (those already using .agents/skills like Codex, OpenCode, and Replit) or requiring compatibility symlinks (like Claude Code using .claude/skills, Cursor using .cursor/skills, etc.).

For non-universal agents, the setup creates relative symlinks from each agent-specific directory to the canonical .agents/skills location. The createAgentSkillSymlinks function handles cross-platform symlink creation, using junctions on Windows and standard symlinks elsewhere. It tracks created, skipped, and failed symlinks and reports results to the user.

The new searchMultiselect prompt component provides an interactive filtered selection UI with a locked section showing universal agents that don't need configuration. The implementation uses raw terminal mode and readline for keypress handling, with features like scroll pagination, search filtering, and visual feedback.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 13, 2026
  • Committed: February 13, 2026

Key contributors

  • balintbrews (Acquia)
  • lauriii (Acquia)

Collaboration

balintbrews opened the issue and committed the implementation on the same day. lauriii made their first commit to the issue fork, contributing to the development. The solution borrows the agent selection UI pattern from Vercel's skills project, adapting it for Drupal Canvas's scaffolding flow.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.
